faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is a High-Yield Savings Account (HYSA)?
faq.a1: A High-Yield Savings Account (HYSA) is a savings account that earns significantly more interest than a traditional savings account. Online banks typically offer HYSAs with APYs of 4–5%, compared to the national average of around 0.46%.
faq.q2: What is APY and how is it different from APR?
faq.a2: APY (Annual Percentage Yield) includes the effect of compounding interest, while APR (Annual Percentage Rate) does not. APY gives you a more accurate picture of your actual annual earnings. Always compare APYs when shopping for savings accounts.
faq.q3: Are online bank savings accounts FDIC insured?
faq.a3: Yes, all banks listed on SavingsYieldHub are FDIC insured. The FDIC insures deposits up to $250,000 per depositor, per institution. This means your money is protected even if the bank fails.
faq.q4: What is a CD (Certificate of Deposit)?
faq.a4: A Certificate of Deposit (CD) is a savings product that holds a fixed amount of money for a fixed period of time (term) and pays a fixed interest rate. CD rates are often higher than HYSA rates but your money is locked in for the term.
faq.q5: What is a Money Market Account?
faq.a5: A Money Market Account (MMA) is a type of savings account that typically offers higher interest rates than regular savings accounts. MMAs often come with check-writing privileges and debit cards, combining features of checking and savings accounts.
